tagline: Calculate paint needed for any room size
keywords: room paint calculator, how much paint for a room, paint gallon calculator
calcFn: var wallArea=2*(length+width)*height; var doorArea=doors*21; var windowArea=windows*15; var paintableArea=wallArea-doorArea-windowArea; var gallonsPerCoat=paintableArea/350; var gallons=Math.ceil(gallonsPerCoat*coats*10)/10; var cost=Math.ceil(gallons)*35; return {wallArea:fmt(wallArea,0)+' sq ft', paintableArea:fmt(paintableArea,0)+' sq ft', gallons:fmt(gallons,1)+' gallons', cost:dollar(cost)};

faq.question: How much paint do I need?
faq.answer: One gallon of paint covers approximately 350 sq ft. Most rooms need 2 coats, so a 12x14 room needs about 2-3 gallons.

formulas: Gallons = (Wall Area - Doors - Windows) × Coats / 350 sq ft

tagline: Calculate wallpaper rolls needed for any room
keywords: wallpaper calculator, how many rolls of wallpaper, wallpaper for room
calcFn: var perimeter=2*(length+width); var wallArea=perimeter*height; var subtract=(doors*21)+(windows*15); var coverArea=wallArea-subtract; var singleRolls=Math.ceil(coverArea/28); var doubleRolls=Math.ceil(singleRolls/2); return {wallArea:fmt(coverArea,0)+' sq ft', singleRolls:singleRolls+' rolls', doubleRolls:doubleRolls+' double rolls'};

faq.question: How many rolls of wallpaper do I need?
faq.answer: A single roll covers about 28 sq ft (accounting for waste). Measure your walls, subtract doors and windows, and divide by 28.

formulas: Rolls = Paintable Wall Area / 28 sq ft per roll

tagline: Calculate tiles and grout needed for floors and backsplashes
keywords: tile calculator, grout calculator, how many tiles do I need, tile coverage
calcFn: var area=areaLength*areaWidth; var tileSizeIn=parseInt(tileSize); var tilesPerSqFt=144/(tileSizeIn*tileSizeIn); var tilesNeeded=Math.ceil(area*tilesPerSqFt*(1+wastePct/100)); var boxes=Math.ceil(tilesNeeded/10); var groutLbs=Math.ceil(area*0.5); return {areaSqFt:fmt(area,0)+' sq ft', tiles:tilesNeeded+' tiles', boxes:boxes+' boxes', groutLbs:groutLbs+' lbs'};

faq.question: How many tiles do I need?
faq.answer: Calculate your area in sq ft, then divide by the tile area. Add 10% for waste from cuts. For diagonal patterns, add 15% waste.

formulas: Tiles = Area (sq ft) × (144 / tile size²) × (1 + waste %)

tagline: Calculate fence posts, rails, and pickets needed
keywords: fence calculator, fence material, how many fence posts, fence cost
calcFn: var sections=Math.ceil(totalFeet/postSpacing); var posts=sections+1; var rails=sections*2; var pickets=Math.ceil(totalFeet/(3.5/12)); var concreteBags=posts*2; var postCost=posts*12; var railCost=rails*8; var picketCost=pickets*2.5; var concreteCost=concreteBags*5; var cost=postCost+railCost+picketCost+concreteCost; return {posts:posts+' posts', rails:rails+' rails', pickets:pickets+' pickets', concrete:concreteBags+' bags', cost:dollar(cost)};

faq.question: How many fence posts do I need?
faq.answer: Divide total length by post spacing (typically 6-8 feet), then add 1. For a 100-foot fence with 8-foot spacing, you need 14 posts.

tagline: Calculate insulation needed for walls, attics, and floors
keywords: insulation calculator, R-value calculator, how much insulation do I need, attic insulation
calcFn: var recs={attic:{1:30,3:38,4:49,5:49,6:60},wall:{1:13,3:15,4:21,5:21,6:21},floor:{1:13,3:19,4:25,5:30,6:30}}; var rValue=recs[application][location]; var fiberglassInches=rValue/3.2; var blownInInches=rValue/2.5; var rollsNeeded=Math.ceil(area/48); var costPerSqFt=rValue*0.05; var cost=area*costPerSqFt; return {rValue:'R-'+rValue, fiberglass:fmt(fiberglassInches,1)+' inches', blownIn:fmt(blownInInches,1)+' inches', rolls:rollsNeeded+' rolls', cost:dollar(cost)+' (material only)'};

faq.question: What R-value do I need?
faq.answer: It depends on your climate zone and application. Attics in cold climates need R-49 to R-60. Walls typically need R-13 to R-21. Check Energy Star recommendations for your specific zone.

tagline: Calculate epoxy resin needed for garage and basement floors
keywords: epoxy floor calculator, garage floor epoxy, epoxy coverage, floor coating calculator
calcFn: var area=length*width; var gallonsPerCoat=area/250; var totalGallons=gallonsPerCoat*coats; var kits=Math.ceil(totalGallons/2.5); var cost=kits*120; return {area:fmt(area,0)+' sq ft', gallons:fmt(totalGallons,1)+' gallons', kitSize:kits+' kits', cost:dollar(cost)};

faq.question: How much epoxy do I need for a garage?
faq.answer: Epoxy covers about 250 sq ft per gallon. A standard 2-car garage (400 sq ft) with 2 coats needs about 3.2 gallons or 2 kits.

formulas: Gallons = Floor Area / 250 sq ft per gallon × Coats

tagline: Calculate tile needed for kitchen backsplash
keywords: backsplash calculator, kitchen backsplash tile, backsplash material
calcFn: var area=width*(height/12); var tileSizes={'3x6':18,'4x4':16,'2x2':4,'4x12':48}; var tileArea=tileSizes[tileSize]; var tilesPerSqFt=144/tileArea; var tiles=Math.ceil(area*tilesPerSqFt*1.1); var sheets=tileSize==='2x2'?Math.ceil(area/1):0; return {area:fmt(area,1)+' sq ft', tiles:tiles+' tiles', sheets:sheets>0?sheets+' sheets':'N/A (individual tiles)'};

faq.question: How much tile for a backsplash?
faq.answer: A standard kitchen backsplash is 10-15 linear feet × 18 inches tall = 15-22.5 sq ft. Order 10-15% extra for cuts and waste.

tagline: Calculate stair dimensions to meet building codes
keywords: stair calculator, stair rise and run, stair riser height, staircase calculator
calcFn: var risers=Math.ceil(totalRise/maxRiser); var riserHeight=totalRise/risers; var treads=risers-1; var totalRun=treads*treadDepth; var angle=Math.atan(totalRise/(totalRun))*180/Math.PI; var sumCheck=riserHeight+treadDepth; var codeOk=riserHeight<=7.75&&treadDepth>=10&&sumCheck>=17&&sumCheck<=18.5; return {risers:risers+' risers, '+(risers-1)+' treads', riserHeight:fmt(riserHeight,2)+' inches', totalRun:fmt(totalRun,1)+' inches ('+fmt(totalRun/12,1)+' ft)', angle:fmt(angle,1)+'°', codeCheck:codeOk?'Passes IRC code':'Check local code — may need adjustment'};

faq.question: What is the ideal stair riser height?
faq.answer: Building code allows 4-7.75 inch risers with 10+ inch treads. The most comfortable stairs have 7-7.5 inch risers with 10-11 inch treads.

formulas: Risers = Total Rise / Max Riser Height (rounded up)

tagline: Calculate joint compound needed for drywall finishing
keywords: drywall mud calculator, joint compound, drywall finishing, how much mud for drywall
calcFn: var sqFt=sheets*32; var linearFt=sheets*12; var gallonsPerCoat=sqFt/250; var gallons=gallonsPerCoat*coats*1.2; var buckets=Math.ceil(gallons/4.5); var tape=linearFt; return {sqFt:fmt(sqFt,0)+' sq ft', gallons:fmt(gallons,1)+' gallons', buckets:buckets+' bucket'+(buckets>1?'s':''), tape:fmt(tape,0)+' ft'};

faq.question: How much drywall mud do I need?
faq.answer: One 5-gallon bucket covers about 250 sq ft per coat. For 3 coats on 20 sheets (640 sq ft), you need about 2-3 buckets.

tagline: Calculate crown molding needed with miter angles
keywords: crown molding calculator, molding length, miter angle, trim calculator
calcFn: var perimeter=2*(roomLength+roomWidth); var moldingFt=perimeter*1.1; var pieces=Math.ceil(moldingFt/8); return {perimeter:fmt(perimeter,0)+' ft', molding:fmt(moldingFt,1)+' linear ft', pieces:pieces+' pieces (8 ft)', miterAngle:'45° miter + compound bevel'};

faq.question: How much crown molding do I need?
faq.answer: Measure the room perimeter and add 10% for waste and cuts. For a 12×14 room, the perimeter is 52 ft — order about 57 ft (8 pieces of 8-ft molding).

tagline: Calculate proper wire gauge for electrical circuits
keywords: wire size calculator, wire gauge calculator, AWG calculator, electrical wire sizing
calcFn: var v=parseInt(voltage); var cmil=amps*distance*2*10.8/(v*maxDrop/100); var gauges=[{awg:'14',cmil:4107,amps15:true},{awg:'12',cmil:6530,amps20:true},{awg:'10',cmil:10380,amps30:true},{awg:'8',cmil:16510},{awg:'6',cmil:26240},{awg:'4',cmil:41740},{awg:'2',cmil:66360},{awg:'1/0',cmil:105600},{awg:'2/0',cmil:133100},{awg:'4/0',cmil:211600}]; var selected=gauges[gauges.length-1]; for(var i=0;i<gauges.length;i++){if(gauges[i].cmil>=cmil){selected=gauges[i];break;}} var actualDrop=(amps*distance*2*10.8)/(selected.cmil*v)*100; var wireType=amps<=20?'NM-B (Romex)':amps<=60?'NM-B or THHN':'THHN in conduit'; return {wireGauge:'AWG '+selected.awg, voltageDrop:fmt(v*actualDrop/100,2)+' V', dropPct:fmt(actualDrop,2)+'%', wireType:wireType};

faq.question: What wire size do I need?
faq.answer: For 15A circuits: 14 AWG. For 20A: 12 AWG. For 30A: 10 AWG. Add wire size for long runs to keep voltage drop under 3%.

tagline: Calculate voltage drop over wire runs
keywords: voltage drop calculator, wire voltage loss, electrical voltage drop
calcFn: var cmils={14:4107,12:6530,10:10380,8:16510,6:26240}; var cm=cmils[wireGauge]; var drop=(amps*distance*2*10.8)/cm; var dropPct=(drop/voltage)*100; var delivered=voltage-drop; var ok=dropPct<=3?'Yes — under 3%':dropPct<=5?'Marginal — 3-5%':'No — exceeds 5%, use larger wire'; return {drop:fmt(drop,2)+' V', dropPct:fmt(dropPct,2)+'%', delivered:fmt(delivered,1)+' V', ok:ok};

faq.question: What voltage drop is acceptable?
faq.answer: NEC recommends no more than 3% voltage drop on branch circuits and 5% total (feeder + branch). Excessive drop causes dim lights, overheating motors, and wasted energy.

tagline: Calculate the correct circuit breaker size for your load
keywords: breaker size calculator, circuit breaker sizing, electrical load calculator, amp breaker
calcFn: var v=parseInt(voltage); var amps=totalWatts/v; var designAmps=continuous==='yes'?amps/0.8:amps; var sizes=[15,20,30,40,50,60]; var breakerSize=15; for(var i=0;i<sizes.length;i++){if(sizes[i]>=designAmps){breakerSize=sizes[i];break;}} var wireSizes={15:'14 AWG',20:'12 AWG',30:'10 AWG',40:'8 AWG',50:'6 AWG',60:'4 AWG'}; var note=continuous==='yes'?'Continuous loads derated to 80% of breaker rating':'Standard load — breaker sized to load'; return {amps:fmt(amps,1)+' A', breakerSize:breakerSize+'A breaker', wireSize:wireSizes[breakerSize], note:note};

faq.question: How do I size a circuit breaker?
faq.answer: Divide total watts by voltage to get amps. For continuous loads (running 3+ hours), the breaker must be rated at 125% of the load (or load must be 80% of breaker rating).

tagline: Calculate the correct resistor for LED circuits
keywords: LED resistor calculator, resistor for LED, LED circuit calculator
calcFn: var totalLedV=ledV*ledCount; var resistance=(supplyV-totalLedV)/(ledMA/1000); var powerW=Math.pow(ledMA/1000,2)*resistance; var stdValues=[10,22,33,47,68,100,150,220,330,470,680,1000,1500,2200,3300,4700]; var nearest=stdValues[0]; for(var i=0;i<stdValues.length;i++){if(Math.abs(stdValues[i]-resistance)<Math.abs(nearest-resistance)){nearest=stdValues[i];}} var minPower=powerW<0.125?'1/8W':powerW<0.25?'1/4W':powerW<0.5?'1/2W':'1W'; return {resistance:fmt(resistance,0)+' Ω', nearestStd:nearest+' Ω', powerW:fmt(powerW*1000,1)+' mW', minPowerRating:minPower+' or higher'};

faq.question: How do I calculate a resistor for an LED?
faq.answer: R = (Supply Voltage - LED Voltage) / LED Current. For a 12V supply with a 2V red LED at 20mA: R = (12-2)/0.020 = 500Ω.

tagline: Convert between amps, watts, and volts
keywords: amps to watts, watts to amps, electrical conversion, power calculator
calcFn: var result,formula,at240; if(convertType==='ampsToWatts'){result=value*voltage; formula=value+'A × '+voltage+'V = '+fmt(result,0)+'W'; at240=value*240; return {result:fmt(result,0)+' Watts', formula:formula, at240:fmt(at240,0)+'W at 240V'};}else{result=value/voltage; formula=value+'W / '+voltage+'V = '+fmt(result,2)+'A'; at240=value/240; return {result:fmt(result,2)+' Amps', formula:formula, at240:fmt(at240,2)+'A at 240V'};}

faq.question: How do I convert amps to watts?
faq.answer: Watts = Amps × Volts. For example, a 15A circuit at 120V provides 1,800 watts of power.

tagline: Calculate real, reactive, and apparent power
keywords: power factor calculator, kVA to kW, reactive power, apparent power
calcFn: var apparentPower=realPower/powerFactor; var reactivePower=Math.sqrt(apparentPower*apparentPower-realPower*realPower); var angle=Math.acos(powerFactor)*180/Math.PI; var rating=powerFactor>=0.95?'Excellent':powerFactor>=0.85?'Good':powerFactor>=0.7?'Fair — consider correction':'Poor — correction needed'; return {apparentPower:fmt(apparentPower,2)+' kVA', reactivePower:fmt(reactivePower,2)+' kVAR', pfAngle:fmt(angle,1)+'°', efficiency:rating};

faq.question: What is power factor?
faq.answer: Power factor is the ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA). A PF of 1.0 is perfect efficiency. Most commercial buildings have PF of 0.80-0.95. Low PF means wasted energy.

formulas: Apparent Power (kVA) = Real Power (kW) / Power Factor

tagline: Calculate the right pipe diameter for water supply
keywords: pipe size calculator, water pipe diameter, plumbing pipe sizing, pipe flow
calcFn: var flowCFS=flowGPM/448.83; var minArea=flowCFS/velocity; var minDia=Math.sqrt(minArea*4/Math.PI)*12; var sizes=[0.5,0.75,1,1.25,1.5,2]; var nominal=sizes[sizes.length-1]; for(var i=0;i<sizes.length;i++){if(sizes[i]>=minDia){nominal=sizes[i];break;}} var actualArea=Math.PI*Math.pow(nominal/12/2,2); var actualVel=flowCFS/actualArea; return {minDiameter:fmt(minDia,2)+' inches', nominal:nominal+' inch', velocity:fmt(actualVel,1)+' ft/s'};

faq.question: What size pipe do I need?
faq.answer: For typical home: 3/4 inch main, 1/2 inch branches. For higher flow (multiple fixtures simultaneously), use 1 inch main. Pipe size is determined by flow rate and maximum velocity (usually 5-8 ft/s).

formulas: Diameter = sqrt(4 × Flow Rate / (π × Max Velocity))

tagline: Calculate water flow rate from pipe size and pressure
keywords: water flow rate calculator, GPM calculator, pipe flow rate, water pressure flow
calcFn: var dia=parseFloat(pipeDia); var area=Math.PI*Math.pow(dia/2/12,2); var frictionFactor=0.02; var headLoss=frictionFactor*length*8/(dia/12); var effectivePressure=Math.max(pressure-headLoss*0.433,5); var velocity=Math.sqrt(2*effectivePressure*144/62.4)*0.6; var flowCFS=velocity*area; var flowGPM=flowCFS*448.83; var dailyGal=flowGPM*60*24; return {flowGPM:fmt(flowGPM,1)+' GPM', velocity:fmt(velocity,1)+' ft/s', dailyGal:fmt(dailyGal,0)+' gal/day'};

faq.question: How do I calculate water flow rate?
faq.answer: Flow rate depends on pipe diameter, water pressure, and pipe length. For most homes, 3/4 inch pipe at 50 PSI provides 8-12 GPM flow.

formulas: Velocity depends on pressure head and friction losses

tagline: Calculate the right water heater capacity for your home
keywords: water heater size calculator, how big water heater, water heater capacity, gallon water heater
calcFn: var perPerson={low:8,medium:12,high:18}; var peakGPH=people*perPerson[usage]; var tankSize=peakGPH<=30?30:peakGPH<=40?40:peakGPH<=50?50:peakGPH<=65?65:80; var tanklessGPM=peakGPH/20; var monthlyGas=tankSize*0.7; return {peakGPH:peakGPH+' gallons/hour', tankSize:tankSize+' gallon tank', tankless:fmt(tanklessGPM,1)+' GPM tankless', monthlyGas:dollar(monthlyGas)+'/month'};

faq.question: What size water heater do I need?
faq.answer: For 1-2 people: 30-40 gallon tank. For 3-4 people: 40-50 gallon. For 5+ people: 50-80 gallon. Tankless units are sized by flow rate (GPM), not storage.

formulas: Peak Demand = People × Gallons per Person per Peak Hour

tagline: Calculate the correct slope for drain pipes
keywords: drain slope calculator, pipe slope, drain pipe fall, plumbing slope
calcFn: var dia=parseFloat(pipeDia); var slopePerFt=dia<=2?0.25:0.125; var totalDrop=slopePerFt*length; var grade=(slopePerFt/12)*100; return {slopePerFt:slopePerFt+' inches per foot', totalDrop:fmt(totalDrop,2)+' inches ('+fmt(totalDrop/12,2)+' ft)', dropInches:fmt(totalDrop,1)+' inches', grade:fmt(grade,2)+'%'};

faq.question: What slope do drain pipes need?
faq.answer: Pipes 3 inches and larger need 1/8 inch per foot minimum. Pipes under 3 inches need 1/4 inch per foot. Too little slope causes clogs; too much and water outruns solids.

formulas: Total Drop = Slope per Foot × Pipe Length

tagline: Calculate water pressure from elevation and pipe properties
keywords: water pressure calculator, water pressure PSI, head pressure, elevation pressure
calcFn: var densities={water:62.4,salt:64.1}; var d=densities[density]; var psi=elevation*d/144; var kpa=psi*6.895; return {psi:fmt(psi,1)+' PSI', kpa:fmt(kpa,1)+' kPa', head:elevation+' ft of head'};

faq.question: How do I calculate water pressure from height?
faq.answer: Water exerts 0.433 PSI per foot of elevation. A water tower 100 feet high provides about 43 PSI at the base.

tagline: Calculate PEX pipe, fittings, and manifold connections
keywords: PEX pipe calculator, PEX plumbing, PEX fittings, PEX sizing
calcFn: var totalPipe; var fittings; if(homeRun==='homerun'){totalPipe=fixtures*avgDistance; fittings=fixtures*2;}else{totalPipe=fixtures*avgDistance*0.6; fittings=fixtures*4;} totalPipe=totalPipe*1.1; var mainSize=fixtures>6?'1 inch':'3/4 inch'; var branchSize='1/2 inch'; return {totalPipe:fmt(totalPipe,0)+' ft', mainSize:mainSize, branchSize:branchSize, fittings:fittings+' fittings (approx)'};

faq.question: Should I use home-run or trunk-and-branch PEX?
faq.answer: Home run uses more pipe but gives better pressure and easier isolation. Trunk and branch uses less pipe but shares flow between fixtures. Home run is preferred for new construction.

formulas: Home Run: Total Pipe = Fixtures × Avg Distance × 1.1

tagline: Estimate flight costs between cities
keywords: flight cost calculator, airplane ticket estimator, flight price, how much to fly
calcFn: var baseCPM=distance<500?0.20:distance<1500?0.12:distance<3000?0.10:0.08; var classMultiplier={economy:1,premium:1.7,business:3.5,first:6}; var timingMultiplier={lastmin:1.8,short:1.3,advance:1,early:0.9}; var oneWay=distance*baseCPM*classMultiplier[class]*timingMultiplier[timing]; oneWay=Math.max(oneWay,49); var roundTrip=oneWay*1.8; var perMile=oneWay/distance; return {estimate:dollar(oneWay), roundTrip:dollar(roundTrip), perMile:'$'+fmt(perMile,3)+'/mile'};

faq.question: How much does a flight cost?
faq.answer: Domestic economy flights average $0.08-0.20 per mile depending on distance and timing. A 1,500-mile cross-country flight typically costs $150-350 one way in economy.

formulas: Estimate = Distance × Base Cost/Mile × Class Multiplier × Timing

tagline: Plan your trip budget with daily cost estimates
keywords: travel budget calculator, trip cost planner, vacation budget, travel cost estimator
calcFn: var dailyRates={budget:75,medium:150,high:300,luxury:500}; var daily=dailyRates[destination]; var totalDaily=daily*travelers; var tripDailyTotal=totalDaily*days; var flightTotal=flightCost*travelers; var tripTotal=tripDailyTotal+flightTotal; var perPerson=tripTotal/travelers; return {dailyPerPerson:dollar(daily)+'/day', totalDaily:dollar(totalDaily)+'/day', flightTotal:dollar(flightTotal), tripTotal:dollar(tripTotal), perPersonTotal:dollar(perPerson)};

faq.question: How much should I budget for a vacation?
faq.answer: Budget travel: $75/day per person. Mid-range: $150/day. Upscale: $300/day. These include accommodation, food, transportation, and activities. Add flights separately.

formulas: Trip Total = (Daily Rate × Travelers × Days) + (Flight Cost × Travelers)

tagline: Convert times between world time zones
keywords: time zone converter, time zone calculator, what time is it in, world clock
calcFn: var diff=parseFloat(toZone)-parseFloat(fromZone); var newHour=hour+diff; var dayShift='Same day'; if(newHour>=24){newHour-=24;dayShift='Next day';}else if(newHour<0){newHour+=24;dayShift='Previous day';} var h=Math.floor(newHour); var m=Math.round((newHour-h)*60); var timeStr=h.toString().padStart(2,'0')+':'+(m>0?m.toString().padStart(2,'0'):'00'); var period=h>=12?'PM':'AM'; var h12=h>12?h-12:(h===0?12:h); return {convertedTime:h12+':'+(m>0?m.toString().padStart(2,'0'):'00')+' '+period+' ('+timeStr+')', difference:(diff>=0?'+':'')+diff+' hours', dayShift:dayShift};

faq.question: How do time zones work?
faq.answer: The world is divided into 24 time zones, each roughly 15° of longitude. UTC (GMT) is the baseline. Zones east are ahead (+), zones west are behind (-). Some zones use half-hour offsets (India at UTC+5:30).

formulas: New Time = Original Time + (To Zone - From Zone)

tagline: Estimate jet lag recovery time based on time zones crossed
keywords: jet lag calculator, jet lag recovery, time zone adjustment, travel fatigue
calcFn: var daysPerZone=direction==='east'?1.5:1; var recoveryDays=Math.ceil(zonesCrossed*daysPerZone); var adjustHours=zonesCrossed; var tip=direction==='east'?'Start going to bed 30 min earlier each night a few days before travel. Seek morning sunlight at destination.':'Stay up until local bedtime on arrival. Seek afternoon/evening sunlight.'; return {recoveryDays:recoveryDays+' days', adjustHours:adjustHours+' hour body clock shift', tip:tip};

faq.question: How long does jet lag last?
faq.answer: Rule of thumb: 1 day per timezone crossed going east, slightly less going west. A flight from NY to London (5 zones east) takes about 5-7 days for full adjustment.

tagline: Calculate fuel cost for road trips
keywords: road trip gas calculator, fuel cost calculator, trip gas cost, driving cost
calcFn: var gallons=distance/mpg; var fuelCost=gallons*gasPrice; var costPerMile=fuelCost/distance; var roundTrip=fuelCost*2; return {gallons:fmt(gallons,1)+' gallons', fuelCost:dollar(fuelCost), costPerMile:'$'+fmt(costPerMile,3)+'/mile', roundTrip:dollar(roundTrip)};

faq.question: How do I calculate gas cost for a trip?
faq.answer: Divide total miles by your car's MPG to get gallons needed, then multiply by gas price. A 500-mile trip at 28 MPG with $3.50/gal gas costs about $62.50.

tagline: Calculate damage per second for any game
keywords: DPS calculator, damage per second, game damage calculator, DPS formula
calcFn: var baseDPS=damage*attackSpeed; var critMult=1+((critChance/100)*(critDamage-1)); var avgHit=damage*critMult; var effectiveDPS=avgHit*attackSpeed; var critDPS=effectiveDPS-baseDPS; return {baseDPS:fmt(baseDPS,1), effectiveDPS:fmt(effectiveDPS,1), critDPS:'+'+fmt(critDPS,1)+' from crits', avgHit:fmt(avgHit,1)};

faq.question: How is DPS calculated?
faq.answer: Base DPS = Damage × Attack Speed. With crits: Effective DPS = Base DPS × (1 + Crit Chance × (Crit Multiplier - 1)). This gives average damage over time including critical hits.

tagline: Calculate your chances of getting rare drops
keywords: loot drop calculator, drop rate calculator, rare drop probability, RNG calculator
calcFn: var p=dropRate/100; var noDropAll=Math.pow(1-p,attempts); var atLeastOne=(1-noDropAll)*100; var expected=attempts*p; var for50=Math.ceil(Math.log(0.5)/Math.log(1-p)); var for99=Math.ceil(Math.log(0.01)/Math.log(1-p)); return {atLeastOne:fmt(atLeastOne,2)+'%', expected:fmt(expected,2)+' drops', for50pct:for50+' attempts', for99pct:for99+' attempts'};

faq.question: How many tries for a 1% drop?
faq.answer: 69 attempts for 50% chance, 230 for 90%, 460 for 99%. The formula is attempts = ln(1-target) / ln(1-droprate). Even at 1% drop rate, it is NOT guaranteed in 100 tries.

tagline: Check if your CPU or GPU is bottlenecking performance
keywords: PC bottleneck calculator, CPU GPU bottleneck, gaming bottleneck, PC performance
calcFn: var cpuScore={budget:40,mid:70,high:90,top:100}; var gpuScore={budget:40,mid:60,high:80,top:100}; var resolutionWeight={1080:0.6,1440:0.4,2160:0.2}; var cpuWeight=resolutionWeight[resolution]; var gpuWeight=1-cpuWeight; var cpuS=cpuScore[cpuTier]; var gpuS=gpuScore[gpuTier]; var cpuImpact=cpuS*cpuWeight; var gpuImpact=gpuS*gpuWeight; var bottleneck=cpuImpact<gpuImpact?'CPU':'GPU'; var diff=Math.abs(cpuImpact-gpuImpact); var severity=diff<10?'Balanced (minimal bottleneck)':diff<25?'Mild '+bottleneck+' bottleneck':'Significant '+bottleneck+' bottleneck'; var fps=Math.round((cpuImpact+gpuImpact)*0.9); var rec=diff<10?'Good balance — no upgrade needed':bottleneck==='CPU'?'Upgrade CPU for better performance':'Upgrade GPU for better performance'; return {bottleneck:bottleneck+' limited', severity:severity, recommendation:rec, estimatedFPS:fps+' FPS (approximate)'};

faq.question: How do I know if my PC is bottlenecked?
faq.answer: If GPU usage is below 90% while gaming, your CPU is likely the bottleneck. At higher resolutions (4K), the GPU becomes more important; at 1080p, CPU matters more.

formulas: Bottleneck analysis considers CPU tier, GPU tier, and resolution

tagline: Estimate download time for games based on file size and speed
keywords: download time calculator, game download speed, how long to download, internet speed
calcFn: var fileMB=fileSize*1024; var speedMBps=speed/8; var seconds=fileMB/speedMBps; var mins=seconds/60; var atPeak=mins/0.8; var hours=Math.floor(mins/60); var remainMins=Math.round(mins%60); var peakHours=Math.floor(atPeak/60); var peakMins=Math.round(atPeak%60); var timeStr=hours>0?hours+'h '+remainMins+'m':remainMins+' min'; var peakStr=peakHours>0?peakHours+'h '+peakMins+'m':peakMins+' min'; return {time:timeStr, atPeak:peakStr+' (realistic)', dataUsed:fileSize+' GB'};

faq.question: How long does a 70GB game take to download?
faq.answer: At 100 Mbps: about 95 minutes. At 50 Mbps: about 3 hours. At 25 Mbps: about 6 hours. Real speeds are usually 70-80% of your plan speed.

tagline: Convert mouse sensitivity between games
keywords: mouse sensitivity converter, DPI calculator, eDPI, gaming mouse sensitivity
calcFn: var edpi=dpi*gameSens; var multipliers={csgo:1,valorant:3.18,apex:1/3.18,overwatch:10.6,fortnite:2.5}; var converted=gameSens/multipliers[targetGame]; var cm360=360/(edpi*0.022); return {edpi:fmt(edpi,0), converted:fmt(converted,4)+' in-game sens', cm360:fmt(cm360,1)+' cm/360°'};

faq.question: What eDPI do pros use?
faq.answer: Most CS2 pros use 600-1200 eDPI (DPI × sensitivity). Valorant pros typically use 200-400 eDPI (equivalent after conversion). Lower sensitivity = more precision but requires more desk space.

tagline: Estimate Twitch streamer income from subs and ads
keywords: Twitch earnings, Twitch income calculator, streamer money, how much do streamers make
calcFn: var subRevenue=subscribers*2.50; var monthlyHours=hoursPerWeek*4.33; var adImpressions=avgViewers*monthlyHours*2; var adRevenue=(adImpressions/1000)*adRate; var bitsRevenue=avgViewers*0.5; var total=subRevenue+adRevenue+bitsRevenue; var perHour=total/monthlyHours; return {subRevenue:dollar(subRevenue), adRevenue:dollar(adRevenue), bitsRevenue:dollar(bitsRevenue), totalMonthly:dollar(total), perHour:dollar(perHour)+'/hour'};

faq.question: How much do Twitch streamers make?
faq.answer: Small streamers (50-100 avg viewers) might make $200-500/month from subs and ads. Streamers with 1,000+ viewers can earn $5,000-20,000/month. Top streamers earn $50,000+/month.
